AccomplishedActivities: [CLICK TRIP DETAIL BUTTON above to see more videos and details] - Today we all arrived in Athens. There are 5 from Texas, 5 from Wisconsin, and 2 from Colorado. It is a nice group. We gathered in our AirBNBs and then walked (and trained) to Mars Hill. There we took in the site and then gathered for devotional and communion. It was my pleasure to lead the conversation and communinion tonight.
GodMoments: We witnessed an incredible sunset on the hill. We also had open discussion about how so many of us have different faith traditionas, but we share so much in common with one another.
Challenges: A diverse of of people in age, occupation, geography, church demonination and backgrounds.
SharedPrayerRequests: That the team will come together and meld. We have some great opportunities ahead of us that will be even greater if we manage to bond quickly.

Pictures.Caption: Goodbye to my Little Piece of Heaven (Nafplio)
Still 45 minutes from Athens and the changes are evident. We are heading to big city.
The team is completely together. We journeyed to Mars Hill and I got to teach tonight as well as lead the team in communion. What a blessing.
The view of the Acroplis from Mars Hill. This was most likely the view that Paul had during his sermon.

PlannedActivities: Play soccer with Sudanese refugees in our socks. Share food and the gospel with them. Climb the Acropolis in Athens.
AccomplishedActivities: Today we traveled, by train, to the Sudanese Refugee encampment called Malakasa near Sfendali, Greece. It was about a 1 hour trip. This encampment is primarily male refugees looking for temporary or permenate EU passports (their papers). They live in shipping containers and have very little. We played Soccer (futbol) with them for 5 hours. They all have different financial means, which means some have shoes and some do not. So, everyone who played, had to play in only socks to level the playing field. It was a great day. In the morning we climbed the hill and stairs of the Acropolis to see the Parthenon and other Greek ruins.
GodMoments: Seeing the men play soccer together. It truly is beyond the language barrier. I am not sure there is anything on Earth more universal than Soccer/Football. Many say that love the international language (I don't disagree that we all experience love and can understand one another at the level), but Soccer is just as universal. All you need are 2 or more people, a bag, stuffing (sockes, cotton, weeds, trash, or a real ball), and two sticks and you can play soccer with someone and not be able to speak a single shared word. (Except everyone seems to know how to call for the ball to be passed to them. LOL)
Challenges: It was warm on the pitch. Several of the guys got blisters. Always wanting to help people more.
SharedPrayerRequests: That God opens the heart of the men we met today to know His son, our Lord and Savior Jesus.

Videos.Title: The Team and The Refugees - Drills

Pictures.Caption: Our team from Wisconsin, Colorado, and Texas. Back Row (left to right): Ellen, Brent, Brent, Josh, Hannah, Zoey, Matt and Bryan. Front Row: Michael, Amelia, and Emma
View of Athens walking up to the Acropolis
The ancient Roman theater built in 161 AD named Odeon of Herodes Atticus. This theater is still used today. If you look close, you can see people working to set up a concernt for tonight.
A view of the back side of the theater with the Parthenon looming over head.

Some kind of fruit that was picked from a tree near where we played soccer today. One of the locals picked it and put it in his hat and handed it out to us. It was delicious. If you know what it was, please let me know.
Some of the Sudanese Refugees listening to instructions on how we are going to play today, along with Josh and Matt from the team.

PlannedActivities: We will meet with children (homeless and refugee) and just play games with them. Show them the love of Christ through games. We will feed them at the end. In the evening we are going on a Homeless walk. The is where Arturo leads us to visit many homeless in our area. We hand out sandwiches, juice boxes, and other items. I will say that this activity literallly and obsolutely changed my life last year. Look for my sermon entitled The Least of These where I talk about the lady and the Red Juice Box.
AccomplishedActivities: We had a fantastic day, but it of course came with its challenges. First, we needed to be at Stavros Niarchos Park at 10 AM. The plan was to the Metro train at 9 AM, which we did. As I started investigating, it came to my attention that the 911 bus we needed to take at the end of the train ride does not run on Saturday mornings. So, the options were to take Taxi's, to take multiple buses, or to walk. We collectively decided to walk the 2.7 KM to the park. It was a nice walk and it was early in the morning, so we were still fresh. We met the kids, teens, young adults, and parents of the Porta Albanian Church of Athens. We played kickball, soccer, tag, frisbee, and many other games for the next 4 hours. We ate pizza together and then decided to walk and tour the park and nearby Library. At this point we already had 14,000 steps and getting tired. We walked to the library and then a local who was with us said no problem we will go to the tram to get back to the train station. BTW, it was hot. We walked to the Tram and waited 12 minutes for the tram. It came and was completely packed with people. The locals on the tram had no interest in letting us on and I am not sure we could have put the 10 of us on it. So, we decided to walk the 2 miles back to the train station. Did I mention it was hot. We walked for the next hour and finally got on the train. We made it back to the apartment and Arturo, from the homeless ministry, met us there to guide us on the homeless evening walk. (That is where we take sandwiches to homeless people sleeping on the streets.) So, we walked for another 2 hours. Needless to say, we walked 30,000+ steps.
GodMoments: The Albanians were such fun and we loved on them and enjoyed this time and serving. The homeless walk touched our hearts. We handed out 75 sandwiches and candy bars. One group specifically reallly touched our hearts. They was a dad and 2 kids. They were on a busy downtown sidewalk/street. The dad was leaning up against the wall on a very hot pavement. (It was 7:45 PM). In his arms was what appeared to be a 4 year child and laying on the pavement next to him was another child about 6 or 7. They were completely asleep. At first our hearts were broken to see this and as Pastor Michael quoted, God, this should not be. All of us cried, but then God spoke straight into me. He instantly showed me the beauty of this family embracing and meeting each others needs. I found myself being stuck inbetween the world of dispair and joy at the same moment. It is so difficult to explain how you can weep for someone and find joy in them at the same time. THANK YOU GOD!!!
Challenges: Sore feet!!! LOL - But really being here in the moment, serving others, and keeping the mentality that just because we are here to help and serve, it does not make us better than those we are serving. As we humble ourselves to serve, we sometimes get puffed up and lose humility. Then superiority kicks in and we place ourselves as better than them. It is at this point that we realize we are THE LEAST of THESE as well.
SharedPrayerRequests: Continued growth, good sleep, hearts to serve and to see people who so desperately want to be seen.

After our walk, we had dinner in the beautiful restaurant. Everyone of us felt very odd to be eating like this when we were just on the streets giving out sandwiches to people and we are not in this space.
This is an image of a man (father) with his 4 year old and 6 year old children. We all wept at the sight, but I was able to find the beauty in a father protecting his children.
Our team with the Albanian kids.

PlannedActivities: Attend a local church service and Homeless Walk
AccomplishedActivities: Today we went to church and worshipped with the Albanians members of La Porta Church in Athens, Greece. We sang and learned and took communion and sang some more and fellowshipped and ate a great meal. We love the Albanian Church and its lovely members. After church we came back to our apartment and had a really nice debrief time with the entire team. A lot of us opened up, some of us cried, we laughed, and got to know each other better. We did our devotional after the debrief time and we realized that our open debrief discussion actually covered the topics of the devo. God is truely GOOD!!! After devo we made more sandwiches and went on another Homeless Walk. We saw some of the same people and some new ones. Last night, I had the pleasure to meet John from England. He and I talked for about 15 minutes. He shared a lot about himself and I was able to pray with him. I was not supposed to go on the homeless walk again tonight, but I wanted to see John again. I wanted him to know that someone wanted to come see him again. I actually got seperated from the team, but I found John on my own. I sat and talked with him for over 45 minutes. He shared so many things about himself and about his journey. He shared some very personal and intimate details. I was so blessed by our time. When I finally had to leave, he asked me to come back if I can, but he understood if I could not. I told him to take care of himself. He said, taking care of himself is easy, the loneliness is not!!! What an image into the lives of the homeless and lost refugees in Athens Greece.
GodMoments: The worship this morning, the love of that church. And the time I spent with John. Also coming together as a team in our debrief and devotional time today.
Challenges: Energy and not being able to help as much as we wish we could and as much as the people of Athens need us.
SharedPrayerRequests: Please pray for our trip to Corinth tomorrow and for my new friend, John in the Park.

AccomplishedActivities:
​Shadows of Empire and the Crossroads of the World: ​Standing among the ruins of Corinth today, you can almost feel the heavy, ambitious hand of Rome. The guide reminded us of just how interconnected this ancient world truly was. Take the Roman Colosseum, for instance—an iconic monument funded entirely by the spoils and gold stripped from the Fall of the Jerusalem Temple.

The geopolitical currents of the day ran deep, and Corinth was right at the center of them.

​Because of its unique geography, it became a bustling bottleneck of humanity; nearly all ships and trade routes had to pass through Corinth. It was too valuable a strategic point to leave empty. Recognizing this, Julius Caesar ordered Corinth to be reestablished as a Roman city in 44 BC (Mummius destroying the old Greek city in 146 BC, leaving it a ghost town) remarkably, this was just a few days before his infamous assassination.

Later down the line, Caesar’s influence would stretch into the medical world too—passing a law that if a woman had fatal complications during childbirth, physicians had to cut her open to try and save the child. We still call it a Caesarean section to this day.

​The Stones Cry Out: Echoes of the New Testament

​As we walked the ancient pavement, the pages of the Bible suddenly felt incredibly tangible. The guide stopped us to look at a specific, weathered piece of stone. Inscribed on it was the name Erastus.

In the New Testament, Erastus is mentioned three times—notably by Paul as the city’s treasurer. His name comes from Eros, hinting at a meaning like the one who is loved in that manner, a common naming convention that strongly suggests he was a former slave who rose to high office. Right there on the plaza, the Latin inscription boldly states that Erastus paved this very area using his own money to give back to the city. Seeing it brings new weight to the words of Jesus: If you deny me, even the stones will cry out. The stones in Corinth are indeed crying out, confirming history.

​It hits hard when you contrast Erastus's wealth with the grim economy of his past. In biblical times, the average price of a human being bought into slavery was just thirty pieces of silver. The exact price Judas accepted to betray Christ.

​A Different Kind of God

​The most beautiful part of the day was how the guide contrasted pagan Roman culture with the revolutionary message of the Gospel.

​In the ancient pagan world, statues often depicted a shepherd carrying a sheep on his shoulders. But that sheep was being carried to an altar for sacrifice—to appease a distant, demanding deity. When Jesus uses that same imagery, He flips it entirely. He carries the sheep not to slaughter it, but to save it.

​That shift changes everything. It’s exactly why we love Him. In ancient times, pagans feared, bargained with, or admired Zeus—but they never said they loved him. Love requires relationship, and relationship requires sacrifice from the top down.

​The guide left us with a question that perfectly sums up the wonder of the day, standing at the crossroads of ancient history:

​How many people will die for their Gods? But, how many Gods will die for you?
GodMoments: The lessons of Paul shared through our guide James. Hidden moments of contention and resolution.
Challenges: Fatigue and group dynamics. Sometimes the overwhelming feeling of guilt when you realize how much we have and how little others have.
SharedPrayerRequests: Pray for the people of Athens. Pray for our family back home. Pray that God will continue the work in us that he has started throughout all of his creation.

Pictures.Caption: Our tour guide in Corinth - James. He is an amazing Pauline Scholar. What a joy to see Corinth with him again this year.
Name plate with Erastus mentioned in the June 8th update. This shows and proves that the Christian religion is and was not for the poor alone, it is for us all.
Temple of Apollo

The Bema seat where Gaius (governor of Corinth) over saw Paul's trial. There is so much proof of this event happening. Paul stood right here, and would have been tied to this beating post if he had been guilty. Paul was beatened several times through his life. Every time he was not guilty but he rarely used his Roman citizenship to avoid a beating. Some believe he didn't use his Roman citizenship beacause of the guilt he felt for executing so many Christians.
Josh was asking if he got the sunscreen rubbed in completely - He hadn't.
View of the Gulf of Corinth from where we ate lunch today.
Back in Athens, this is the actual basketball court where Giannis Antetokounmpo grew up and learned to play basketball. He is now an NBA champion and multi-time MVP.

PlannedActivities: Work at the Refugee Center and the New Start Ministries
AccomplishedActivities: Today we started with a fantastic devotional of hearing Tyler's (local Missionary from Jefferson City, MO) testimony. We then moved over to the Glocal Refugee Cafe and helped served over 150 refugees throughout the day. All the men on the team then went to New Start (a ministry trying to free women from sex trafficking and helping to put on the hearts of men to stop engaging in this). we handed out tracks to men walking the streets near the over 200 brothels in the one neighborhood where public brothels are legal. These brothels are called open door. Basically anyone can walk up to the door, push it open and walk in. We stood near some of the entrances and saw several men walk in and shop for their right woman. It was horrible. At these brothels, men only need to spend 10 Euros for every 15 minutes. In Greece, it is honorable to be a male client at a brothel (in some circles) and disgraceful to be a prostitute. There are somethings I guess this American brain will never understand.
GodMoments: We met Dimitrius today. He is the founder of the New Start Ministry in Athens. Through people like him, he helps me see how we can make a difference. It is so easy to see big issues in the world and think what good would it do if I tried to help. I can't fix this problem. Well, people like Dimitrius show us that you are not called to fix the problem. You are called to do the actions God calls you to do. If it is to love on people in the hopes that just one may someday find Jesus, then that is what you do. I can't fix sex trafficing, but I can help people stuck in it get out, and I can maybe show one participant of this horrific practice, that this is not what human life is about.
Challenges: To see the participants-clients of prostitutes as people too. We have to see them as children of God. Sure, they are not acting like it, but they are worthy of our efforts to help them see the errors in their ways and to start a journey toward Christ. It is easy to just say they are disgusting human-beings and ignore them, but seeing their actions reminds me that we are all broken and all in need of our saviour. Their need may be more visually obvious as they step into a brothel, but our brokeness is like filthy rags as well.
SharedPrayerRequests: Pray for Tyler and his ministry - Prayer for Ahmed, Hussein, and George along with all the refugees they help. In 2025 they had 95 different nationalities come visit their center and seek help. Their center offers coffee, tea, snacks, medical care, social services, games, support, conversation, and hope for men, women, and children - Pray for Dimitrius and New Start, but more inmportantly pray for the 1,000s of women who are forced into prostitution in Athens and pray that the men who abuse them can find their way to redemption and salvation through Our Lord Jesus Christ.

Pictures.Caption: Inside the New Start office with Dimitrius preparing to go on our first walk.

Almost everywhere you look in Athens, you see graffiti. Right on the edge of one of the most densely populated brothel district in all of Europe is this beautiful mural on the side of the building. You can see from the balconies that this is at least 60 feet high
The roof top areas of the Glocal Cafe for Refugees in Athens.
One of the very rare nice statements of graffiti in the city.
Right in the middle of a very exclusive and expensive shopping district, there is this ancient tiny church.
It is near closing time and the busiest shopping street in Athens is still packed.
We had a ping pong tournament today on the Glocal Cafe rooftop. These are the first, second, and third place winners.
A little fun to end this deep day. We found a statue in Corinth of our fearless leader, Bryan Doyle.

PlannedActivities: Work at the Refugee Center and the New Start Ministries
AccomplishedActivities: Today had a similar agenda to yesterday. We served at the Glocal Cafe for Refugees and we went to the New Start ministries for a brothel prayer walk. We ended the day at one of our apartments for a team dinner made by Ty, Brent (Jones), and Michael. They made us a authentic(ish) Mexican Taco dinner. YUMMY!!!! At the cafe we had some great conversations with people from many different countries and had the opportunity to share Jesus with some of them (this is a Swedish Government sponsored program and we are not to proselytize unless they ask us first). We took several of them to a park to play games, have a picnic, and just hang out for 3 hours. After that we headed back over to the New Start Ministries building. Dimitrius shared with us several stories about the brothel industry. There are pictures below that show some detailed information. It is a heavy thing to be a part of and to witness the men going in and out of these brothels. Pray for the women stuck in this industry and pray for the men who have never been introduced to a relationship with Jesus that would convict them of this behavior.
GodMoments: Seeing the smiles of refugees who have nothing, but can sit back and enjoy a good conversation (even if it is through google translate). Witnessing Dimitrius' heart for serving those who no one even wants to believe are alive.
Challenges: Team dynamics are always a challenge. Seeing each persons gifts and growth is a blessing and a challenge. Sometimes we have to be in a role to support others and put our own ambitions aside.
SharedPrayerRequests: A personal one for me today, I am running on empty from a physical and emotional stand point. Please pray for me that I can not only find the energy and strength, but I can put on the light of Jesus to everyone.

Pictures.Caption: The team during our morning devotional.

Bryan, Michael, and Brent (Kern) talking with a refugee from Sudan. He was getting close to getting his EU passport and one night while sleeping on the streets, all of his papers were stolen and he is basically having to start over the process.
Handing out new(ish) clothes to the refugees on the rooftop of the Glocal Cafe for Refugees.
Refugees from at least 43 different countries sit around Natalie during some time at the park. They all really loved to draw and color.
A picture of most of the group that went to the park.
A street near Omonia (a section of Athens). There are about 50 brothels within 4 blocks of this picture.

Every red circle represents a location where there is at least 1 brothel and sometimes 4 or 5 in the same multi-story building. The blue #1 is near where our AirBNB apartment is. The blue #2 is near where Glocal Cafe is. There are at least 400 brothels within 4 miles of this location. This is the Victory Square district and it is one of the most populated brothel districts in Europe.
This is another distric near Omonia where there is a large concentration of brothels.
An abstract mural in the Onomia district.

This mural was painted in 2006 when Greece was going through a very difficult time which include national bankruptcy. The story behind the mural is, when people pray we put our hands toether and lift them to God. These are God's hands pleading for the people of Greece to repent and change their ways. This mural is right on the border of one of the largest brothel districts in Europe.

PlannedActivities: Work at the Refugee Center - Part 3
AccomplishedActivities: Today we worked again at the refugee center. I started out by shopping throughout the Omonia and Monastiraki Districts looking for a patio umbrella for the Glocal Refugee Center. I looked in 100s of shops, but had no luck. I did some research online and found a store that looked like they carried this. I call Bryan and he came and picked me up on this car. We drove to Jumbo Store and found the umbrella pictured below. We helped the staff at the center paint a room and we spent most of the day hanging out with refugees. Most of the team visited the Olympic Stadium (the one from the early 1900's). We went to dinner at a nice Chinese food restaurant and then most of the team met at a bar to watch the first World Cup Match.
GodMoments: Finding the umbrella was a labor of love and it paid off in the end. God helped us get the Center a nice tool for shade.
Challenges: Painting a room with minimal tools, high ceilings, and limited time. Ellen and I had quite a time trying to find the Chinese Restaurant.
SharedPrayerRequests: Keep us safe tomorrow as we travel to Aegina Island for a day off and then safe travels back to the States this weekend.

AccomplishedActivities: Today we traveled to the beautiful island of Aegina for some rest and fun before we start our journeys home. Michael, Josh, Hannah, Emma, and Matt will start their journey to Wisconsin at 1 AM. Brent J, Amelia, and Ellen will start their journeys at 3:30 am. Bryan will start his at 10:30 am. Zoie at 12 PM. Ty will fly out at 6 PM, and Brent K will fly out at 10:30 am on Sunday June 14. A lot of moving parts over the next 36 hours. Aegina was wonderful. Ty and I rented motor scooters and let many of our friends take turns driving them. We had our traditional last night together dinner. While we eat, each person must close their eyes and the leader picks 4 people to say something about them. Then the person opens their eyes and hears what is said about them from their peers on the team. It is a special and beautiful time. We traveled by Ferry today and the weather was very nice. Storms around the area all day and tonight, but no bad weather on us the entire trip.
GodMoments: Josh and Hannah got engaged. It was a great celebration. We had our final dinner and God showed up in many ways. Noboday got injured the enitre time we were in Athens. AMEN!!!
Challenges: Saying goodbye. Listening to the team tell you things and not sure what they are going to say.
SharedPrayerRequests: Please travel for safe travels. For me alone one more night. For me driving the car in Athens. For tired people to get the rest that is needed.
